one_line_takeaway: AGMark improves LVLM watermarking by replacing VLA-Mark's static one-shot visual-importance estimate with a per-step, attention-plus-context-guided, entropy/density-adaptive protected-token budget, yielding better text quality and CHAIR hallucination scores alongside higher paraphrase-attack robustness, at the cost of extra hyperparameter tuning and modest latency overhead.
key_finding: On Llava-Next/AMBER, AGMark beats the strongest text-quality baseline MorphMark: perplexity 4.80 vs 4.98 (a 0.18 reduction) and BLEU 31.88% vs 26.92% (a 4.96-point improvement), while detectability stays high (AUC 99.91%, Accuracy 99.50%). Across all three backbones and both datasets, AGMark's detectability AUC is at least 99.36% and accuracy at least 96.22%. Under the five text-space removal attacks, AGMark's largest AUC drop is 11.28% (down to 88.61% AUC) under paraphrasing -- smaller than VLA-Mark's 26.36% drop (to 73.35%), MorphMark's 16.46% drop (to 83.28%), and IE's 13.25% drop (to 86.46%) under the same attack; under synonym-substitution the AUC drop is at most 0.87%, versus 3.34% (VLA-Mark), 2.08% (MorphMark), and 3.55% (IE). Sample-wise CHAIR hallucination mean is 6.2% for AGMark versus 7.9% and 8.2% for the next two best methods (IE, SynthID). Ablations show attention-guiding is the single most important weight-extraction component (removing it drops BLEU by 13.94 points and STS by 3.80, the worst of any single-component ablation among w/o Attention/Vision/Context), while removing the entire adaptive vocabulary-partitioning module (FixedScale) causes the largest CHAIR degradation (+2.20 points) among the partitioning ablations. Latency overhead is modest: on Qwen3-VL, AGMark adds about 0.41s versus the unwatermarked baseline for a 200-token/10-image generation run (21.80s vs 21.39s), roughly comparable to VLA-Mark (21.59s) and higher than lightweight KGW (21.52s).
core_contribution: AGMark is a logits-based watermarking framework for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that, at every decoding step, dynamically identifies which vocabulary tokens are 'semantic-critical' (i.e., strongly tied to the image content and to the ongoing text context) and adaptively resizes the protected-token subset before green/red vocabulary partitioning. It has two components: (A) Semantic Critical Weight Extracting, which computes a vision critical weight (via the model's own last-layer attention over visual tokens, cosine-similarity-weighted against vocabulary embeddings) and a context critical weight (cosine similarity between the last hidden state and vocabulary embeddings), standardizes both, and fuses them via a convex combination controlled by hyperparameter omega; and (B) Adaptive Vocabulary Partitioning, which uses token entropy (uncertainty) and 'weight density' (how concentrated the semantic-critical-weight distribution is, computed via a top-p-style minimal covering subset) to set a per-step ratio eta_t of tokens that get swapped into the green list ahead of the standard KGW-style logit bias. The stated goal is to fix two limitations of the prior LVLM watermark VLA-Mark: (1) VLA-Mark computes vision-critical weights only once (static/one-shot) rather than per decoding step, and (2) VLA-Mark ignores the density of the weight distribution when deciding how many tokens to protect, both of which the authors argue cause semantic misalignment late in generation and low-quality long-tail token protection.
threat_model: This is a provenance/IP-protection (watermarking) paper, not an attack paper. The 'defender' is the LVLM provider who wants to embed an imperceptible, statistically detectable signal into generated captions/responses for content traceability and intellectual-property protection, using a secret key as in the standard KGW logits-based paradigm (red/green vocabulary lists plus a logit bias delta). The adversary is someone trying to remove/evade the watermark via post-hoc text manipulation without access to the secret key or model internals: the paper evaluates five black-box text-space removal attacks -- random word insertion, deletion, synonym substitution, paraphrasing, and translation -- applied to the first 200 generated samples. It explicitly does not evaluate white-box or query-based 'informed' attacks such as watermark stealing (cited in Related Work as future/out-of-scope: those methods 'assume essentially unlimited access to the watermarked model, which limits their practicality').
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) a clear detectability-vs-generation-quality trade-off governed by alpha and tau -- larger alpha/tau protect more semantic-critical tokens (improving CHAIR/perplexity/BLEU) but shrink the effective watermark signaling space, reducing AUC/accuracy (e.g., accuracy drops to 98.88% at alpha=0.29 versus 99.50% at alpha=0.27); (2) the omega fusion weight has a non-monotonic effect on quality, meaning it must be tuned rather than set a priori -- both too-small and too-large omega degrade fluency (perplexity 5.17 at omega=0.30, 4.82 at omega=0.60, versus 4.80 at the chosen omega=0.50); (3) modest but nonzero inference latency overhead versus lightweight baselines like KGW; (4) the largest single robustness weakness is paraphrasing attacks, where AGMark's AUC drop (11.28%) is smaller than most baselines but still the single biggest degradation across all five attack types tested, and it is only evaluated against black-box text-manipulation attacks, not informed/white-box attacks such as watermark stealing, which the Related Work section flags as a known threat class left unaddressed. Not stated as a limitation but notable from the data: on Qwen3-VL, AGMark's own gains over VLA-Mark on BLEU/AUC are much smaller (e.g. AUC 99.36% vs VLA-Mark 99.05%, only marginally ahead) than on Llava-Next, indicating backbone-dependent effect size.

quantitative_results: Detectability AUC at least 99.36% across three LVLMs (Llava-Next-Llama3, Qwen3-VL, InternVL-3.5) and two datasets (AMBER, MS-COCO); accuracy at least 96.22%
Robustness AUC at least 88.61% under paraphrasing attack (the worst-case attack), versus VLA-Mark 73.35%, MorphMark 83.28%, IE 86.46% under the same attack
Llava-Next/AMBER: AGMark perplexity 4.80 vs MorphMark (2nd best) 4.98; BLEU 31.88% vs MorphMark 26.92%; BertScore 91.14 and STS 91.85 (only method exceeding 91 on both)
Sample-wise mean CHAIR hallucination: AGMark 6.2% vs IE 7.9% vs SynthID 8.2%

Ablation: removing attention-guiding (w/o Attention) drops BLEU by 13.94 points and STS by 3.80 (largest single-component degradation in weight-extraction ablations); removing the whole adaptive partitioning module (FixedScale) raises CHAIR by 2.20 points (worst among partitioning ablations)

Hyperparameter sweep on Llava-Next/AMBER: omega=0.50 (chosen) gives AUC 99.91%, CHAIR 6.20%, perplexity 4.80; alpha=0.29 gives best CHAIR (5.90%) and perplexity (4.78%) but lower accuracy (98.88%) than alpha=0.27 (99.50%)
Inference latency (10 images, 200 tokens): AGMark 21.80s on Qwen3-VL vs 21.39s unwatermarked (+0.41s), vs VLA-Mark 21.59s and KGW 21.52s
